title: Why Self-Care Isn't Selfish: Dr. Laura Brayton on Holistic Motherhood
guestBio: Dr. Laura Brayton is a chiropractor, pediatric specialist, and functional medicine practitioner with over 20 years of practice in Hoboken, NJ. She hosts the Well-Adjusted Mama podcast and specializes in empowering women through conscious conception, healthy pregnancy, and joyful motherhood using holistic approaches including the Webster Technique for optimal birth outcomes.
excerpt: Dr. Laura Brayton reveals why self-care is mandatory for mothers, not optional. From preconception detox to nervous system regulation, discover how holistic motherhood creates the foundation for everything else in your life.
metaDescription: Dr. Laura Brayton shares why self-care is mandatory for moms, chiropractic care benefits in pregnancy, and preconception detox essentials.
keyTakeaways: Self-care is not selfish—it's mandatory for every mother because everyone benefits when mom is well.
Chiropractic care is safe throughout all three trimesters and can significantly improve pregnancy outcomes.
Preconception detox should begin six months before conception for both partners to optimize fertility and baby's health.
Your nervous system must be regulated to lead from intuition rather than fear-based decisions.
Life is written in pencil—you have permission to pivot your career to align with motherhood.
Being fully present for one hour daily gives your children more attention than most kids receive.
Corporate skills translate beautifully into household leadership when you embrace the shift in energy.

pullQuotes.text: Self-care is not selfish—it is mandatory for every mother
Life is written in pencil—you have permission to pivot
Being present one hour a day is more than most kids are getting right now

timestamps.label: Why chiropractic care is essential during pregnancy
The Webster Technique for smoother natural birth
Masculine vs. feminine energy and motherhood
Self-care is not selfish — it is mandatory

content:
What if everything you've been told about self-care being selfish is completely wrong? What if taking care of yourself isn't just beneficial—it's actually mandatory for being the mother and leader you want to be?

In this powerful conversation, Dr. Laura Brayton, chiropractor and host of the Well-Adjusted Mama podcast, challenges every assumption about motherhood, wellness, and what it truly means to lead from a place of strength. With over 20 years of practice helping women through conception, pregnancy, and beyond, Dr. Laura brings both clinical expertise and lived experience to the conversation about holistic motherhood.

The Foundation of Everything: Why Your Nervous System Matters

You've heard about nervous system regulation, but Dr. Laura explains why it's particularly crucial for mothers and women in leadership. When your nervous system is dysregulated, you're operating from fear rather than intuition. You're making decisions from a place of survival rather than wisdom. This shows up everywhere—in how you parent, how you lead your team, and how you show up for yourself.

Dr. Laura emphasizes that being well-adjusted goes far beyond physical alignment. It's about having a nervous system that allows you to respond rather than react, to lead from clarity rather than chaos. When you're regulated, you can access that inner knowing that guides you toward the right decisions for your family and your career.

Chiropractic Care: Your Secret Weapon for Pregnancy

If you're pregnant or planning to be, Dr. Laura's insights about chiropractic care during pregnancy will revolutionize how you think about prenatal wellness. The Webster Technique, which she specializes in, focuses on aligning the pelvis to give baby optimal space to get into the best position for birth. This isn't just about comfort during pregnancy—it's about setting the stage for smoother labor and delivery.

What's remarkable is how safe and beneficial chiropractic care is throughout all three trimesters. Dr. Laura sees women experience reduced pregnancy symptoms, better sleep, and more energy when their spine and nervous system are properly supported. For high-achieving women who are used to pushing through discomfort, this represents a fundamental shift toward supporting your body rather than overriding its signals.

Preconception: The Six-Month Game Changer

Here's something that might surprise you: the health choices you and your partner make in the six months before conception matter more than you realize. Dr. Laura advocates for preconception detox that goes beyond just taking prenatal vitamins. This is about reducing environmental toxin load, optimizing nutrition, and creating the healthiest possible environment for conception.

This preparation phase isn't just about the mom-to-be. Partners need to be involved too, because sperm health is equally important for fertility and the baby's wellbeing. It's a radical shift from the traditional approach of waiting until you're already pregnant to start thinking about health optimization.

From Corporate Warrior to Nurturing Mother: The Energy Shift

One of the most profound parts of this conversation centers on the energy shift required when transitioning from high-powered career woman to mother. Dr. Laura speaks candidly about how the masculine energy that serves you well in corporate settings—the drive, the pushing, the constant doing—needs to be balanced with feminine energy in motherhood.

This doesn't mean abandoning your ambitious nature or your leadership skills. Instead, it's about recognizing that motherhood requires a different kind of strength. Your corporate skills absolutely translate into being an amazing household CEO, but the energy behind those skills might need to soften.

Dr. Laura offers a powerful reframe: life is written in pencil. You have permission to pivot, to change course, to prioritize differently than you did before children. This isn't failure or giving up—it's conscious choice-making based on what matters most to you now.

The Mandatory Nature of Self-Care

Perhaps the most revolutionary message Dr. Laura shares is this: self-care is not selfish, it's mandatory. When you take care of yourself, everyone benefits. Your children get a more present, regulated mother. Your partner gets a more connected spouse. Your team gets a clearer, more focused leader.

This isn't about elaborate spa days or expensive retreats—though those can be wonderful. It's about the daily practices that keep your nervous system regulated and your energy sustainable. It's about recognizing that you can't pour from an empty cup, and that filling your cup isn't optional—it's essential.

For busy entrepreneurs and working mothers, Dr. Laura offers a perspective-shifting insight: being fully present for one hour a day gives your children more quality attention than most kids are receiving. It's not about quantity of time—it's about the quality of your presence when you are with them.

Leading from Intuition, Not Fear

Throughout this conversation, a central theme emerges about leading from intuition rather than fear. When your nervous system is regulated and you're taking care of yourself, you can access that inner wisdom that knows what's right for your family. You're not making decisions based on what others expect or what you think you should do—you're choosing based on what truly serves your highest good.

This is particularly powerful for women in leadership positions who are used to analyzing every decision. Dr. Laura reminds us that sometimes the best decisions come not from spreadsheets and pros-and-cons lists, but from that quiet inner knowing that emerges when we're truly well-adjusted in every sense of the word.

The conversation with Dr. Laura Brayton offers a roadmap for approaching motherhood and leadership from a place of wholeness rather than depletion. It's an invitation to see self-care not as luxury but as leadership, and to recognize that taking exquisite care of yourself is one of the most generous things you can do for everyone in your life.

quiz.title: What's Your Motherhood Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Inspired by Episode 56 with Dr. Laura Brayton — discover how you naturally lead in motherhood and where your nervous system might be asking for more support.

questions.question: When your morning routine gets completely derailed, you typically...

options.text: Push through and power on — there's no time to stop
Feel the frustration but try to adapt in the moment
Pause, take a breath, and reset your intentions
Let go of the plan entirely and follow what feels right

questions.question: Your idea of self-care looks most like...

options.text: What self-care? I'll rest when everything's done
A quick workout or podcast when I can squeeze it in
Intentional time for journaling, meditation, or therapy
Whatever my body is asking for that day — naps, walks, baths

questions.question: When someone asks 'How are you doing, really?' you...

options.text: Give the highlight reel — you don't want to burden anyone
Share a little but keep it practical, not emotional
Get honest — vulnerability is how you connect
Check in with yourself first because you might not actually know

questions.question: Your relationship with your body during motherhood feels...

options.text: Like a machine I need to keep running efficiently
Complicated — I appreciate it but don't always listen to it
Like a partnership I'm actively working to strengthen
Sacred — my body has done extraordinary things and deserves reverence

questions.question: When it comes to asking for help, you...

options.text: Rarely do — it's faster to handle it yourself
Will ask when you're truly stuck, but not before
Are learning to ask proactively, even when you don't 'need' to
See receiving help as a form of self-love, not weakness

questions.question: The leadership lesson motherhood has taught you most is...

options.text: How to be incredibly efficient with limited resources
How to stay calm when plans fall apart
How to lead yourself before you lead anyone else
How to surrender control and trust the process

results.title: The Powerhouse
results.description: You lead with strength, discipline, and relentless energy. You're the mom who gets it ALL done — but Dr. Laura Brayton's message about nervous system regulation is especially for you. Your body is keeping score even when your mind says 'I'm fine.' This episode is your permission slip to slow down without guilt.

results.title: The Steady Navigator
results.description: You're adaptable, practical, and resilient. You roll with the punches better than most — but you might be absorbing more stress than you realize. Dr. Brayton's insights on holistic wellness can help you move from 'managing' to truly thriving. You don't just deserve to cope — you deserve to flourish.

results.title: The Conscious Leader
results.description: You're already doing the inner work, and it shows. You lead with self-awareness and emotional intelligence — two of the most powerful leadership tools that exist. This episode will deepen your practice and validate what you already know: taking care of yourself IS taking care of everyone around you.

results.title: The Intuitive Mother
results.description: You trust your instincts, honor your body, and lead from a place of deep knowing. You embody the holistic approach Dr. Brayton describes. Your growth edge? Helping other women see that your way isn't 'soft' — it's actually the most sustainable form of leadership there is.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Today's guest is someone I connected with instantly, not just as a professional, but as a woman deeply committed to helping mothers feel supported and empowered in their bodies and lives. Dr. Laura Brayton is a doctor of chiropractic, pediatric specialist, and the host of the Well Adjusted Mama podcast. Through her work, she has helped thousands of families rethink wellness from the inside out, focusing on nervous system health, holistic motherhood, and creating a strong foundation for both moms and babies.

I recently had the honor of being a guest on her show and what struck me most was how aligned our missions are - helping women step into leadership through the roles society often undervalues. And fun fact, she also introduced me to the author, Chelsea Ann, who many of you heard on my show last week. Today, we're going to talk about motherhood, nervous system health, leadership through care, and what it really means to be well adjusted in today's world. Hi, Laura. So nice to have you here on the show.

**Laura:** Oh, hi, Aimee. I'm excited to be here. Thank you.

**Aimee:** For those meeting you for the first time, what drew you into chiropractic care, specifically working with mothers and children?

**Laura:** Yeah. So I have an interesting story. I have been under chiropractic care my entire life. And it started when my mom was pregnant with me and she was having some lower back pain, which is very common during pregnancy. And my dad was this huge advocate for chiropractic care and had his chiropractor he went to regularly to optimize his nervous system function and to feel good.

And he said to my mom, who had never been to a chiropractor, hey, why don't you go see my chiropractor and get adjusted? So she did. She got a lot of relief. She felt good. She had a vaginal delivery. And then the chiropractor afterwards, after the birth said, bring in your baby to get checked.

So I've been adjusted since I was a baby. Regularly, my entire childhood, my entire family went to the chiropractor and that was really led by my dad being such an advocate for it and educating my mom. And then the two of them together were like, we're gonna raise our children with the chiropractic lifestyle. So my sister and I were so blessed to have that experience. And around five years old I was like, I think I'm gonna be a chiropractor and I just loved babies, I loved pregnant mamas, so I just really felt called to working with that population.

**Aimee:** That's so fun. I had a similar story. When I was a baby, I was about 18 months old and I stopped walking. And my parents were freaked out, my mother especially. And they took me to the chiropractor and my little left hip, I have a little hip dysplasia. So they popped my hip back into place. And ever since then, they've taken me, I started with regular chiropractic care at that point in my life, but very grateful for it. I definitely am. I'm a believer, especially for moms, especially pregnant moms.

**Laura:** My gosh, chiropractic care during pregnancy is so awesome. It's safe all three trimesters. It is really awesome preconception as well, because we really help prepare the body for a healthy conception and to feel good during pregnancy. And with those maintenance adjustments as well, what we see is women have less symptoms like the nausea, the vomiting, constipation, headaches. A lot of these symptoms plus neck and back pain can be safely and non-pharmaceutically resolved with chiropractic adjustments.

And that's the challenge. I think a lot of women, if they were used to like, oh, I have a headache, I'm just going to pop medicine, they're really looking for an alternative. And this is a great way to really create a lifestyle of not taking medications on a regular basis if you don't have to, and finding other alternatives to supporting your health and wellness and really not masking the symptoms, but recognizing we want to treat the underlying cause.

So doing that, then I see, especially in the third trimester, we get mom's pelvis in the good position. Baby gets lined up because the uterus attaches to the front of the sacrum. So when mom's sacrum is out of alignment, which is very common during pregnancy, we could see a breech or transverse position baby, which obviously can interfere with having a vaginal delivery as well. So we get mom lined up, babies lined up, and it's like a basketball going through the hoop. During labor, we see an easier transition during that labor process. It's more effective, it's efficient and less interventions are typically required so then mom can have the vaginal delivery that she was looking to have - less amount of time, easier and safer essentially.

**Aimee:** That's so good and I know you're on the east coast. If a pregnant mom is listening to us, she just needs to look for what kind of chiropractor?

**Laura:** Yeah that's a great question. So anybody who's looking for a chiropractor for pregnancy or postpartum care, as well as for pediatrics, you want to go to the ICPA website. That's the International Chiropractic Pediatric Association. They have Webster certified chiropractors who are trained in adjusting pregnant and postpartum bodies, as well as infants and children. So that's ICPA4kids. And at that website, it has find a chiropractor. And the chiropractors in your zip code have gone through additional training to really take care of that specialized niche practice.

**Aimee:** Yeah, because it really is. It's not something that every single chiropractor knows how to do or is confident with. So your podcast, Well Adjusted Mama, has such a strong following. What does well adjusted really mean, beyond the physical sense?

**Laura:** Yeah, it really does. It's foundationally a physical alignment, right? And that's really coming from my chiropractic philosophy, but I recognize that when we're physically in alignment and our nervous system is operating at its optimal level with that parasympathetic sympathetic nervous system balance, which chiropractic is a piece of, in addition to our meditation and our healthy nutrition, our exercise, our stress management, then we see that we're mentally, emotionally, and spiritually also well-adjusted, right? But if the physical part isn't there, it's really hard to have the other components.

So even if I'm working with a mom, let's say who's having some postpartum baby blues or depression, then we have to look at how we could support her with her physical body, including her biochemistry, because that's going to contribute to her emotional state.

**Aimee:** What do you do with biochemistry? What are you looking at? Are you looking at vitamins?

**Laura:** Well, what we're doing is really boils down to the foundation of the nervous system function. When something's out of alignment in your spine, it's going to interfere with how our brain communicates with the rest of our body. So we want to make sure that neurotransmitter support is optimized. Also, we know a lot of neurotransmitters are produced in our gut. So if there's some leaky gut issues going on, then we want to work on supporting that. So I do bring in supplements as needed, using homeopathy, herbal, nutritional supplements, because those work really synergistically together.

But a lot of it too is also really stress. I think as women, we're really overscheduled. We're really running hard. And even today, I had a pregnant mom in my office who's pregnant with baby number three and was coming in to help turn her breech baby. Today, we found out the baby is head down, which is fantastic. But sadly, her mother passed over the weekend. And so she's dealing with elder care versus having little ones and being pregnant.

This is the reality for a lot of women nowadays, especially as women are entering motherhood a little later in life, that we're really taking care of multi-generations. And it's a lot on ourselves. So we have to recognize how are we getting supported? How are we making sure our needs are met? How are we practicing self-care? Self-care is so important for women, especially as caretakers when we're caring for everyone else around us.

So I do find that that is so important. And when that's happening, we're doing things that help us feel good, then our biochemistry is going to benefit from that, because thoughts impact molecules of emotion - it's a book written by Candace Pert years ago that talks about if we're having positive thoughts and positive self-talk, then we're going to create positive physiology and biochemistry versus the negative, which is the opposite. So a lot of it is really being mindful and there's some cool modalities out there like PSYCH-K, for example, that works on limiting beliefs and recognizing our subconscious minds and how we could be limiting ourselves. And there's hypnotherapy as another way to help access our limiting beliefs and our subconscious mind that might be creating some blocks, but there's so many different ways. EFT is another one, emotional freedom technique. So recognizing and finding a modality that works for you and bringing it into a daily practice, I think is so helpful.

**Aimee:** I love that. What does self-care look like for you?

**Laura:** That's a great question. I have a very busy practice in Hoboken, New Jersey. I've been here for over 20 years. I also now have a virtual functional medicine practice that I launched and I have a husband and eight-year-old son at home. So I'm taking care of a lot of things and juggling a lot of parts, as a mom and an entrepreneur. And an entrepreneur in healthcare is next level of caretaking, right?

So my self-care is always the priority. And so to me, it's about delegating wherever I can. I know you're so good with that, Aimee. It's not about being a control freak. If we try to control and do everything ourselves, we will burn ourselves out. We have to recognize there are things that only I can do and that's what I'm going to stick to and be good at. And anything else, I have an amazing nanny. She does my laundry, she cooks, she cleans, she takes care of my home and my son when I'm at work and God bless her, because that is stuff I don't want to do all that. I mean, I want to be with my son as much as I can, but I don't want to do all those other things if I can be serving at a higher level to a larger amount of women in the world, really through my podcast and my functional medicine practice.

So that is, and asking for help and support, the communication with your partner, I think is really, really important too. And it's like, if you're over giving and then you're feeling depleted and resentful, then you want to have that conversation about where the partner in life can step up and perhaps assist as well or hire as needed.

**Aimee:** Exactly. Yeah, I love that. Those are very wise words. That has definitely been exactly what has worked for me too. When you and I first talked, we felt this deep overlap between nervous system regulation and leadership energy. How do you see those two connected?

**Laura:** Well, I find that when our nervous system is not regulated, when we're in that fight or flight, over sympathetic, run, run, run, we're being chased by a tiger, we are ungrounded and we are really not able to make the best decisions. In a lot of ways, what I also see is people are making decisions based on fear. And there's a lot of fear mongering that's occurring in the media, social media and mainstream media that can be very coercive and really kind of lead us off track in really making the best decisions for ourself and our families.

So as the leaders of our family, as moms, we really need to ground ourselves and really put attention to, like I said, a mindfulness or a meditation practice that keeps us grounded. Because then when there is something in front of us that we have to make a decision on, we're not going from a place of manipulation and fear. That's not where we make our best decisions. When we're really trusting our intuition and as women and as mothers, then we're really going to make the right decision every time. And we can't do that if we're running from a tiger. So it's really getting out of that constant survival mode of fighting and flighting and then just being like, okay, I got this. I trust I know the answer here. And even if this expert in front of me is giving me opposite advice, I'm going to do more research. I'm going to go for a second opinion. I think that's so important.

We really, with the power of the internet, we can really get a lot of information that even moms a couple generations ago did not have access to. And it is empowering because people really used to put their medical doctor up on a pedestal. They were God. What they said is what you did. And it was very finite viewpoint of what health looks like. And now we can really see so much more and we can work with practitioners anywhere in the world that we couldn't even do just a couple of decades ago.

So I think even since I've been in practice, the whole landscape has changed with how we can be empowered with our health, our parenting decisions, listening to podcasts to get information about how we can make choices that we didn't necessarily grow up learning about. So we're really becoming much more educated and then we can follow the people that we resonate with and continue those conversations.

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. Yeah. Way more of a savvy consumer now than we ever were before, which is amazing. We're savvy consumers of health care in a way that we never have been before because of the internet and social media and how much more information we can consume now than we ever had. I love it.

**Laura:** And what I see from that too is things like Make America Healthy Again, like that whole movement is really coming from moms that are demanding better quality food for our families and less toxic chemicals in our environment, and really holding our government responsible to keep us safe because that was really going off the track with approving these toxic chemicals in our food and land and soil that were polluting us and really hurting these next generations and it's not okay. If we're killing our earth and killing everything around us, then what are we leaving for our children? What kind of legacy is left here?

So when we have this awareness that we're not going to tolerate it and we're going to demand stricter legislations around food colors and dyes, and also choosing as a consumer not to buy that crap too, the type of chemicals that we use to clean our homes. No, thank you. We can really recognize that all the marketing that we're getting, we can make a different choice. And the way we spend our money is also driving what these companies are going to produce when people are demanding better quality and cleaner products for skin care, personal care. And then we're going to continue to encourage that production of those products.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely. I think there was an illusion of safety. The FDA created this illusion of safety with a lot of household products that were on the market, with a lot of us not really realizing that we had that extra 10,000 chemicals in America that they didn't have in Europe and Canada. And when I took my first product to market, I was actually shocked that we didn't go through the FDA to get approved. We were just under that GRAS - generally regarded as safe. And it was shocking to me that you can just bring a product to market because in Canada, we had to go through Health Canada. They had to approve every single ingredient, see where it was coming from, and basically go through a year long approval process to sell the product in Canada. And I kind of assumed we had the same thing in America until I went through it. And I went, whoa.

**Laura:** Here, it's really putting the due diligence on the company to check their ingredients and make sure that things are safe when they're putting their product out there. And not every company has our best interest in mind. Some of them, it's just like, how much product can we sell? And they will use low quality ingredients to keep the cost low, to the detriment of the user. So we have to be really educated. We have to read our labels. We have to recognize how things impact our health. I think you really hit it on the head about the savviness. And once again, who's doing the most shopping for the home is the moms, right?

**Aimee:** It's like 80%. I think it's over 80% of the shopping is done by mom.

**Laura:** Yeah, and it could be online, delivery, whatever, more delegation. But the whole point is that we're getting stuff we're really interested in doing things that are cleaner because we know it's going to impact our health. And that's a big part of what I do with my preconception functional medicine as well, is really recognizing how can we not just clean your physical body out now before you get pregnant and your partner as well, but indirectly impact your epigenetics. So that means the things in our environment that are actually changing, for the better or worse, the egg and the sperm before they even conceive a baby. And we can make changes to that. We're going to create a healthier baby and a healthier pregnancy.

So I love working with couples who, six months, ideally before they're even starting to try getting pregnant to start making these lifestyle shifts. If they haven't already looked in their medicine cabinet or looked under their sink in the kitchen to see what kind of products they're using, let's start diving deep there and digging through this and throwing everything out that is not in the interest of you and your future family.

**Aimee:** Totally. That would be like phthalates, parabens, sulfates, those kinds of chemicals that are just nasty.

**Laura:** And they really do interfere with not just the ability to get pregnant, but to have a healthy baby because we'll see women that either are having fertility challenges and that's a component to it, or they're also getting pregnant, but with multiple miscarriages. And a lot of those multiple miscarriages is because the baby is not healthy and the body is discharging it because it's not going to thrive to full term. So we have to really realize why is this happening? Why is fertility hard? The fertility rates are really not going in the right direction. We're having more and more challenges in getting pregnant. This is really, unfortunately, our entire lifestyle that has led to this point. And we have to do our due diligence to take care of ourselves and our partner. Our partner has 50% of the genetics for the baby. So they have a responsibility to clean up their lifestyle as well.

**Aimee:** Well, I didn't know this until recently, but the sperm actually forms the placenta. So dad being healthy is very, very important for the viability of the baby to make it to full term.

**Laura:** Sure. Not to mention just whatever their ability to pass on healthy genetic material and even the ability to impregnate an egg. That's the first step. So we see a lot of people kind of skipping that with IUI or IVF, especially the IVF, and especially when it's unknown reasons. That's concerning to me because there is always a reason for everything. And if it's not like you have a blocked fallopian tube or something that would really make sense why it's hard to conceive naturally, not digging into that can show up later in life is what I'm trying to say.

There are reasons that are going on there from either you, your partner, or both that are creating that challenge. And sometimes, honestly, sometimes it's just stress. That could be it alone. It's just both partners are stressed, especially the woman, and they get stressed about trying to get pregnant. And you never want to be in that stressed, sympathetic state either, because if we go back to our cavewoman times, a cavewoman would never get pregnant if she was running from a tiger. That is not the right time to get pregnant. You do not, you have to survive, but we can't survive in that state.

So we have to recognize that when we're feeling stressed chronically, we're working crazy hours at the office, we're going out and having a glass of wine most nights of the week, we're not fueling our body with healthy nutrition. We're not scheduling our exercise. Why would our bodies want to conceive in that condition? It doesn't make sense. We have to nourish ourselves, our minds, our bodies. We have to have work-life balance. I think that is so important. And that is hard because especially also I see a lot of women, especially in the New York City area where I practice, are very masculine in the way they look at work. And they're extremely high powered, extremely successful, very high level jobs. But those high level jobs that pay well also demand a lot on your body because of their travel, their hours, they're working 60, 70 hours a week plus. And that's a challenge. If you're doing that profession and you loved it in your 20s and now you're like, oh, I want to have a baby. How do you shift out of that? It's a really hard thing to do, to kind of get that work-life balance when you're looking to make a baby.

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. I had Chelsea Ann on my show and we were talking about how women can be out of their nature sometimes with being in that masculine energy when you're in those high power corporate jobs, because those tables were made by men for men. So you have to rise and kind of go into that energy to be in that space and do well in that space. But then if you're trying to get into the whole motherhood, being pregnant, having a baby thing, it is a different kind of, it's a softer energy. There's a lot more softness, grace, ease that needs to come into that mother energy, that calm, that is kind of completely opposite energy to where those women are at.

**Laura:** Chelsea is a good friend of mine. And we've talked so much about this because the reality is it's really hard if you've been doing that for so many years of your life. It's really like, how do I get out of that? It's almost just like that becomes natural. Being masculine becomes your nature because it really serves you in that type of corporate world setting. And yet these women really do want to become mothers. And in some cases that might require a job shift, and recognizing that that position was not your identity. I think that's the other challenging thing. It's like, oh, I'm a partner in this really big law firm. That's my identity. And how do I not continue that? I've worked so hard for this but yet these women are like I want to have two or three babies and I maybe even want to take a few years off of work or whatever.

Or sometimes they have the babies and they just pop right back to work and I just see that these women are not feeling well. That's the bottom line. They're not, I wouldn't consider them thriving. They're surviving. They're doing their professional work and they're having these babies and they love their babies but they're not able to really be present and available to their babies because it's just impossible. We can't do it all at that time. So recognizing sometimes it's okay to take a few years off and go back into the corporate world once your kids are in school or whatever works for you, or shift to a different type of career.

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. And it's okay to pivot and change. I have another friend who wrote a book and it's called Life is Written in Pencil. And I love that because a lot of these women, high power women, we kind of have written life in Sharpie. And this is what I'm going to be. And this is who I am. And the truth is, it's okay to pivot in life. It's okay to make adjustments and change as the seasons change, as the circumstances of life change.

**Laura:** A hundred percent. As I think about that too, it can be really hard if you're somebody like myself, who's invested over a hundred thousand plus dollars in your education to then walk away from that career, because it's like, oh my God, I'm still paying the student loans or whatever. But it's okay. We go through these different life cycles within our life, and we can recognize that like, okay, this is what I want to do now. And we can also come back to it.

I had the honor of meeting with Natalie Grabeau, who is the oldest woman to complete an Ironman, 80 years old in the fall of 2025, when she did a full Ironman in Hawaii at 80 years old, so amazing. And I wanted to learn about her story and she was a stay at home mom until her children were in school, had a professional career before that. And then when her kids were out of the house, she went back to that because it helped keep her mind sharp. And you have to have a competitive brain to be able to do an Ironman at 80 years old. I think there's just, anytime, I don't have any desire to do that. That is a lot of work. I did one marathon. I can't even add on the swimming and the biking part.

So I was so amazed by her, but she was just like, yeah, working in corporate did help her with this competitive aspect. Even though she took, let's say 16 plus years out of the professional world to be a stay at home mom. So I think that's also important. If we stop working out of the home, it doesn't mean we're never going to go back. And it doesn't mean we can't go back to something different. And I do also see that with women in my practice, when they're done with their maternity leave, they're like, I'm going to just take a couple of years off. And then I don't want to go back to what I was doing. It didn't fill me up. It didn't give me purpose. And I want to start my own business or I want to do something else. That is going to be more in alignment with raising my young family and also filling me with joy. So I think that's so important.

And yeah, that Sharpie thing is real because some women it's like, well, I went to school and I did all this grad school and whatever. And I had all these degrees and what do you mean I'm not going to use them? Life changes.

**Aimee:** And you talk about it also with The Manager like it doesn't mean those skills aren't still going to be put to use in a different format. And you're going to use them at home and then you're going to hone those skills too. Everything you learned in the corporate world is going to help you manage your household in a whole nother way. You're going to be an amazing household manager because you could manage high level projects. You're highly organized. And so you'll find in your home, you're going to be using those skills, honing those skills, and then taking them to the next level when it comes to emotional intelligence and HR and culture building and all of the things that we really do in our homes that are these amazing jobs that we really need to take credit for as women.

When you become a mom, you really are taking on another full time, it's not just a full time job. The motherhood job is 24 by 7 by 365. And it is a lot of work. It doesn't mean we don't love our children. We love our children, but we are now the CEO of our home as well. And so if you're a mom, if you're becoming a mother and you're working a corporate job and you are feeling dysregulated, you're feeling burnt out, you're feeling sick, you're getting autoimmune, it's okay to take a look at your life and see if there's some changes you can do and use your eraser. It's written in pencil, it's not written in sharpie. Make the changes so that you can be happy and healthy because all our kids really need are happy healthy moms.

**Laura:** That's 100%. They don't care about the titles. They want a happy healthy mom and really that's a mom that even has energy to want to do stuff with their kids and is able to also be really present with their kids and isn't distracted, isn't, got other things going on with work and text, emails, whatever.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. I always tell people if even if just show up and be present with your kids for one hour a day, it's so much more than any other kids are getting right now because the cell phones have really got us, the algorithms really have us so addicted to the phones as parents right now that we're not giving our kids the kind of attention that children have gotten for generations and generations before. So it's like put your phone away. Give them an hour of undivided attention. Be present and try to enjoy the moments that we get to be fully present with them because they say cute things. It's all the beauty of the moment. Taking that time and allowing your attention and your presence to be in that little window of time where you're just banking those memories of them being whatever age they're at. And they change so quickly. In a blink of an eye, you're like, oh my God, my baby is now going to school. And then I see families are like, oh my God, my child's going to college. It goes by really, really fast.

**Laura:** It goes so fast. Being very present, you're really going to be able to enjoy and remember those moments. And that even slows it down a little bit.

**Laura:** Yeah, I would say I knew a lot about pregnancy and babies before I became a mom. And that was already something I was doing a lot in my podcast. Well Adjusted Mama predates me becoming a mother because it's been around since 2014, which is crazy.

**Laura:** Which I really feel like was because of all the information and empowerment that I had going into that. **Aimee:** Me too. Me too. I was, and I don't believe in circumcision. So for me, it was like, I had, that was something that we had to talk about when we were dating because I don't want to have that fight when I'm pregnant.

**Laura:** That was a big fight for us actually. And we were having that more difficult conversation before we even knew we were having a boy. And I was like, okay, maybe it'll be a girl. I could do that for my own body, but now with our child, he gets to have a say as well.

So I definitely recommend that interview process while you're dating. I've had this conversation with other younger women. I talk to my children about this. I tell them when you're dating, make sure you talk, if you have ideas about how you want to raise your kids, if you want to have home birth, all that stuff, you need to make sure you talk to your girlfriends, boyfriends about that stuff while you're still dating.
